title: Objects — look things up by name
instruction:
Arrays store values by position. Objects store them by **name** (key).

`user["name"]` or `user.name` — both work. Dot notation is cleaner when you know the key name. Bracket notation is needed when the key is dynamic.

Run this.
successMessage: Dot notation for known keys, bracket notation for dynamic keys. user[field] where field is a variable — you can't do that with dots.

title: Adding, updating, deleting
instruction:
Same syntax for adding and updating — JavaScript doesn't care which:

```js
obj.newKey = value;     // add
obj.existingKey = val;  // update
delete obj.key;         // remove
```

Add email, update city, delete isPremium.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Added, updated, deleted. delete removes the key entirely — unlike setting it to null, which keeps the key with an empty value.

title: Computed property names
successMessage: Computed property names are essential when building objects dynamically — form state, query params, API payloads where field names come from variables.

title: Object.keys, values, entries
instruction:
Three essential methods for working with all an object's data:

- `Object.keys(obj)` — array of key names
- `Object.values(obj)` — array of values
- `Object.entries(obj)` — array of `[key, value]` pairs

Print all keys, all values, and then loop over entries to print `key: value`.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Object.entries() + destructuring is the modern way to loop over objects. It's the equivalent of Python's .items().

title: Spread — copy and merge objects
instruction:
Object spread `{...obj}` creates a shallow copy. Merge objects by spreading both:

```js
const merged = { ...defaults, ...overrides };
```

Later keys overwrite earlier ones — so put overrides last.

Merge defaults with user settings.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: User's dark theme and font size 18 overrode the defaults, but lang and notifications came from defaults. This pattern is how every configuration system merges user preferences.

title: Optional chaining on objects
instruction:
From Chapter 5: `?.` prevents crashes when accessing properties on null/undefined.

This is essential for API responses where nested data might be missing.

Safely access deeply nested data with `?.` and provide defaults with `??`.
successMessage: ?. chains safely through every level. response3?.user?.address?.city returns undefined (not an error), then ?? gives the fallback. Three null-safe levels in one expression.

title: Map — when object keys aren't strings
successMessage: Map with object keys — this is impossible with plain objects (objects are stringified to [object Object] as keys). Use Map when keys need to be non-strings.

title: Set — unique values only

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: [...new Set(array)] is the cleanest one-liner for deduplication in JavaScript. Set also has O(1) lookup — checking if a value exists is faster than array.includes() on large datasets.

title: Build a word frequency counter
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `wordFrequency(text)` function that:
1. Splits text into words (lowercase, remove punctuation)
2. Counts each word using a `Map`
3. Returns the top 5 most frequent words as `[word, count]` pairs

Test with: `"the cat sat on the mat and the cat sat on the hat"`
hint: Use text.toLowerCase().split(' ') and a Map to count. Sort entries by value descending, slice top 5.

codeChecks.message: Use a Map to count word frequencies.

successMessage: A word frequency counter using Map — the foundation of search engines, spell checkers, and text analysers. Chapter 8 done — objects and Maps mastered!
